content.text: Effect Timing: After Paint
useEffect
is a
passive
effect — React schedules it to run after the browser has painted the updated DOM to the screen. This asynchronous scheduling is the defining characteristic that separates
useEffect
from
useLayoutEffect
and from synchronous lifecycle methods in class components.

attrs.alt: React render pipeline showing effect timing

content.text: The pipeline above shows the five stages in order.
useEffect

fires at stage 5, after the browser has already displayed the updated UI to the user. This means your effect code never delays the paint — the user sees the new UI immediately, and your effect runs shortly afterward.
The Render Pipeline in Detail
Stage 1 — Render Phase
React calls your component function (or class
render
method). This is a pure computation: React collects the new JSX output and diffs it against the previous virtual DOM tree. No DOM mutations happen here.
Stage 2 — Commit Phase

React applies the computed DOM mutations synchronously. After commit, the real DOM reflects the latest render output, but the browser has not yet painted — the pixels on screen still show the previous frame.
Stage 3 — Layout Effects
useLayoutEffect
callbacks run synchronously after commit, before paint. They can read DOM layout (
getBoundingClientRect
) and make further DOM mutations; because everything happens before paint, the user never sees intermediate states.
Stage 4 — Browser Paint
The browser composites and paints the frame. The user now sees the updated UI. At this point, all synchronous work by React for this render is complete.
Stage 5 — Passive Effects (useEffect)
React flushes queued
useEffect
callbacks. Because these run after paint, they do not block the browser. If an effect calls
setState
, React schedules another render — potentially another paint — rather than synchronously updating the DOM.
Why this order matters:

if your effect triggers a visible change (e.g. fetching data and then rendering it), the user sees a brief flash of the initial empty state before the data appears. This is expected and correct. Use loading states, Suspense, or server-side rendering to manage the perceived latency.
Observing the Timing
You can verify the execution order by logging from each phase:
Implications for State Updates Inside Effects
When an effect calls
setState
, React does not immediately re-render. Instead it schedules a new render. The user
always
sees at least one painted frame with the old state before the effect-driven update appears.
This two-render pattern is normal and expected. The loading state is not a bug — it is the correct behaviour given that effects run after paint.
Why Passive Effects Improve Performance
Running effects after paint keeps the browser unblocked during the critical path from user interaction to updated pixels.
Network requests in useEffect don't delay the first paint.
Analytics and logging don't hold up the UI thread.
Subscriptions and timers are registered asynchronously, after the user already sees the new state.
If the component unmounts before the effect fires (rare but possible with Suspense), the effect is skipped entirely — preventing wasted work.

Contrast this with a synchronous class lifecycle method like componentDidMount: although it also runs after commit, React treats it as synchronous work and does not guarantee paint has occurred before it runs in all React versions. Modern React's
useEffect
explicitly defers to after paint with a well-defined guarantee.
When the After-Paint Timing Is Wrong

The after-paint timing is ideal for most effects, but there are cases where it causes a visible problem: if your effect reads a DOM measurement and then updates state, the user sees a flicker — the initial paint shows one layout, then the state update triggers another paint with the corrected layout.
Effect Batching in Concurrent Mode
In React 18+, effects are scheduled using the browser's
MessageChannel
mechanism — they run in a microtask-like fashion after the browser paints but before it goes idle. React may batch multiple pending state updates from effects within a single synchronous flush.
This batching behaviour means that even if your effects call
setState
multiple times, React renders the component only once with all accumulated state changes applied.
Strict Mode and Double Invocation

React Strict Mode in development intentionally double-invokes effects (mount → cleanup → remount). This does not change the relative timing — both the initial effect and the re-mount effect still fire after their respective paints. The extra cycle tests resilience, not timing.
Choosing Between useEffect and useLayoutEffect
Situation
Hook to use
Fetching data from an API
useEffect
Setting up a WebSocket or subscription
useEffect
Logging, analytics, tracking
useEffect
Registering a global event listener
useEffect
Measuring DOM node dimensions before paint
useLayoutEffect
Positioning a tooltip or popover relative to an anchor
useLayoutEffect
Imperatively focusing an input without flicker
useLayoutEffect
Animating layout changes without a flash of old position
useLayoutEffect
Default to
useEffect
. Switch to
useLayoutEffect
only when you observe a visible flicker caused by the after-paint timing.
Effects and the Browser's Frame Budget
Modern browsers target 60 frames per second, giving each frame approximately 16.67 ms. React's rendering pipeline is designed to fit inside that budget. The key insight is that
useEffect
runs
outside
a frame: React schedules it to run after the browser yields for paint, so the frame completes without being blocked by your effect code.
This separation matters for perceived performance. If you had to run a 50 ms fetch setup inside the paint frame, the UI would jank. Because
useEffect
runs outside the frame, a slow or async effect never causes the browser to drop frames during the current transition.
Multiple Effects and Their Ordering
A component can have any number of
useEffect
calls. React runs them in the order they appear in the component body, which matches hook call order. All effects for a single render batch run in a single passive-effects flush, after paint.

The deterministic ordering means you can rely on effect A completing its registration before effect B runs — though you generally should not design effects that depend on each other's side effects, since that couples them implicitly.
Why Not Run Effects Synchronously After Commit?
React could theoretically run all effects synchronously after commit, before paint — this is exactly what
useLayoutEffect
does. But doing it for
all
effects would be harmful:
Blocking paint:
A network fetch or subscription setup inside a synchronous effect would hold the browser hostage until it completes.
Unnecessary delay:
Most effects (data fetching, analytics, subscriptions) do not need to run before the user sees the UI. Blocking paint for them wastes perceived performance.
Cascading re-renders:
If a synchronous effect calls setState, React would need to re-render synchronously before paint, potentially causing multiple layout passes.
Design principle:
React optimises for the common case. The common case is that most effects are optional post-render work (fetch, log, subscribe).
useLayoutEffect
exists as the explicit escape hatch for the rare case where synchronous post-DOM, pre-paint work is required.
Effect Scheduling with Concurrent Features
In React 18's concurrent renderer, renders can be interrupted and restarted. Effects only run after a render has
committed

— interrupted renders never trigger effects. This means the number of effect runs is always equal to the number of successful commits, regardless of how many times React started and discarded a render.
This guarantee holds even when using
startTransition
to defer low-priority renders. Effects for the deferred render still run after the deferred commit, not during the high-priority render.
Measuring Effect Timing with the Performance API
You can precisely measure when effects run relative to paint by using the browser's Performance API. This is useful for profiling and verifying that a heavy effect is not contributing to jank.
For production monitoring, avoid leaving Performance API marks in production builds. Use a build flag or environment check to restrict them to development only.
Effects and Server-Side Rendering
useEffect

does not run on the server. During server-side rendering (SSR), React renders the component tree to HTML without a browser context, so there is no paint phase and no passive-effects flush. Effects only execute on the client after hydration.
This behaviour is intentional and safe for code that accesses browser-only globals like
window
document
, or
navigator
. Place all browser-only code inside
useEffect
to guarantee it never runs on the server.
Profiling Effects in React DevTools

React DevTools Profiler provides a visual timeline of render phases, effects, and their durations. Opening Profiler, recording a session, and clicking a rendered component reveals a flamegraph showing:
Which components re-rendered and why.
How long the commit phase took.
Which passive effects ran and their approximate duration.
Whether an effect triggered a follow-up render.

If you see effects labeled as 'slow' in the profiler, consider whether the computation inside can be moved out of the effect entirely (perhaps it can be derived state or a memo) or deferred further with a
setTimeout
if it is non-critical.
Deferred Initialization with Empty Dependency Array
Sometimes you genuinely need code to run after paint but you do not need it tied to a specific state variable. The
useEffect
hook with an empty dependency array
fires once after the initial mount. This is the right pattern for one-time initialization that should not block the UI:

The empty dependency array does not mean the effect has no dependencies — it means the effect intentionally runs only once. If you are accessing props or state inside the callback, ESLint's exhaustive-deps rule will warn you to add them as dependencies or restructure the code.
Race Conditions from After-Paint Timing
A common after-paint timing pitfall is the race condition: the user changes a prop (e.g.
userId
) before the previous effect's async operation completes. The old fetch resolves after the new one and overwrites the correct state.
The fix is always in the cleanup function — abort the in-flight operation so its result is discarded before the new effect starts. With
AbortController
and a guard variable, you guarantee that only the most recent effect's result can update state.
Summary
useEffect

fires asynchronously, after the browser has painted the updated UI to the screen. This after-paint timing keeps the UI thread unblocked and the browser responsive, at the cost of an initial render without effect-driven data.
React's render pipeline: render phase -> commit phase -> useLayoutEffect -> browser paint -> useEffect.
useEffect is a passive effect — it never blocks the browser from painting.
State updates inside useEffect trigger additional renders, not synchronous DOM mutations.
React 18 automatically batches multiple setState calls within the same effect flush.
Use useLayoutEffect only when after-paint timing causes a visible flicker from DOM measurement.
Strict Mode runs effects twice in development to surface cleanup bugs — the relative timing is unchanged.
